question: interface vs type — what's the difference?
answer:
Both describe shapes. interface can be re-opened/merged (declaration merging) and is the idiom for object/class contracts. type is more flexible — it can be a union, intersection, tuple, or alias a primitive.

Rule of thumb: interface for public object shapes, type for unions and utility compositions.
tip: Only type can express a union like 'a' | 'b'.
code:
type Status = 'active' | 'inactive';
interface User { id: number; name: string; }

question: any vs unknown vs never.
answer:
• any — turns off type checking (avoid).
  • unknown — a safe any; you must narrow it before use.
  • never — a value that can't exist (a function that always throws, or an exhausted union).
tip: Prefer unknown over any for untyped input like JSON.parse results.
code:
let val: unknown = JSON.parse(str);
if (typeof val === 'string') val.toUpperCase(); // narrowed

question: What are generics?
answer: Generics allow functions, classes, interfaces, and types to work with different data types while preserving type safety. Instead of using any, a generic acts as a placeholder type that is determined when the function or class is used.
tip: Think of generics as 'type parameters'. Use extends to restrict what types are allowed.
deep:
What are Generics?

Generics allow us to write reusable code that works with multiple data types while maintaining compile-time type safety. Instead of writing separate functions for strings, numbers, or custom objects, we write one generic implementation.

// Without Generics
function first(arr: any[]) {
  return arr[0];
}

The above function works, but the return type becomes any, so TypeScript loses type safety.

// With Generics
function first<T>(arr: T[]): T {
  return arr[0];
}

The type T is inferred from the argument passed to the function.

Type Inference

Most of the time, TypeScript automatically infers the generic type.

const name = first(['John', 'Alice']);
// T = string

const age = first([20, 30]);
// T = number

You can also specify it explicitly.

first<string>(['A', 'B']);

Generic Interfaces

Generics are commonly used with interfaces to model reusable data structures.

interface ApiResponse<T> {
  data: T;
  success: boolean;
}

const users: ApiResponse<string[]> = {
  data: ['John', 'Alice'],
  success: true
};

Generic Classes

class Box<T> {
  constructor(public value: T) {}
}

const numBox = new Box(100);
const strBox = new Box('Angular');

Generic Constraints

Sometimes we need to ensure the generic type contains certain properties. This is done using extends.

function printId<T extends { id: number }>(obj: T) {
  console.log(obj.id);
}

Now only objects containing an id property are allowed.

printId({ id: 1, name: 'John' }); // ✅
printId({ name: 'John' });         // ❌ Error

Multiple Generic Types

function pair<K, V>(key: K, value: V) {
  return { key, value };
}

const p = pair('id', 100);

Real-world Angular Example

Angular's HttpClient heavily relies on generics.

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

this.http.get<User[]>('/api/users');

Because of generics, TypeScript knows the response is an array of User objects, enabling IntelliSense and compile-time type checking.

users[0].name; // ✅
users[0].salary; // ❌ Property does not exist

Why not use any?

anyGenerics
Loses type safetyMaintains type safety
No IntelliSenseFull IntelliSense
Errors found at runtimeErrors found during compilation
Not reusable safelyReusable and type-safe

Common Built-in Generic Types

  • Promise<T>
  • Array<T>
  • Map<K, V>
  • Set<T>
  • Observable<T> (Angular/RxJS)

Interview Tip

If asked 'Why use Generics instead of any?', explain that generics preserve the actual type throughout the program, whereas any disables type checking completely. Mention Angular's HttpClient.get<T>() as a real-world example—it's one of the most common follow-up questions.

Interview Answer

"Generics allow us to write reusable, type-safe code by using type parameters instead of fixed types or any. The actual type is supplied when the function or class is used, allowing TypeScript to preserve IntelliSense and compile-time checking. In Angular, generics are commonly used with HttpClient, Observables, Promises, and reusable interfaces like API responses."

question: Name some built-in utility types.
answer:
• Partial<T> — all props optional (patch/update DTOs).
  • Required<T> — all props required.
  • Pick<T,K> / Omit<T,K> — select or exclude keys.
  • Record<K,V> — a map/dictionary type.
  • Readonly<T> — immutable props.
tip: Partial<T> is perfect for update payloads where only some fields change.
code:
type UserPatch = Partial<User>;
type Roles = Record<string, boolean>;

question: What are enums, and what's a union alternative?
answer:
enum defines a set of named constants. Numeric enums generate reverse mappings; string enums are more readable in logs.

Many teams prefer a string literal union (type Size = 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg') — it's lighter and tree-shakable, with no runtime object.
tip: const enums and string-literal unions avoid emitting runtime code.
code: enum Role { Admin = 'ADMIN', User = 'USER' }

question: Explain public, private, protected, readonly.
answer:
• public (default) — accessible anywhere.
  • private — only within the class.
  • protected — within the class and subclasses.
  • readonly — assignable once (in declaration or constructor).
tip: Parameter properties let you declare + assign in the constructor in one line.
code: constructor(private http: HttpClient, readonly id: string) {}

question: What is type narrowing / type guards?
answer: Refining a broad type to a specific one within a block using checks like typeof, instanceof, in, or a custom type predicate (x is Cat). TypeScript then knows the precise type inside that branch.
tip: Custom guards return 'param is Type' to teach the compiler.
code: function isCat(a: Animal): a is Cat { return 'meow' in a; }

question: Union vs intersection types.
answer: Union (A | B) — the value is one of the types. Intersection (A & B) — the value has all properties of both.
tip: Union = OR, intersection = AND.
code:
type Id = string | number;      // union
type Employee = Person & Staff;  // intersection

question: What does strictNullChecks do?
answer: With strictNullChecks on, null and undefined are not assignable to other types unless explicitly included — the compiler forces you to handle empty values, preventing a whole class of runtime errors.
tip: It's part of the recommended strict: true preset — always enable it.
code: let name: string | null = null; // only allowed because of the union

question: What are mapped types?
answer: Types that transform each property of another type. Utility types like Partial and Readonly are mapped types under the hood.
tip: Combine with keyof to iterate a type's keys.
code: type Optional<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };

question: What do keyof and typeof do at the type level?
answer: keyof T produces a union of a type's property names. Type-level typeof value captures the type of an existing value/const so you can derive types from data.
tip: typeof + keyof lets you build types from a constant config object.
code:
const config = { host: '', port: 0 };
type ConfigKey = keyof typeof config; // 'host' | 'port'

question: What are decorators (as used in Angular)?
answer: Decorators are functions that add metadata/behaviour to classes, methods, properties, or parameters. Angular relies on them heavily: @Component, @Injectable, @Input, @Output.
tip: They require experimentalDecorators / emitDecoratorMetadata in tsconfig (Angular sets this up).
code:
@Input() label = '';
@Output() change = new EventEmitter();

question: What does 'as const' do?
answer: A const assertion makes a value deeply readonly and narrows it to its literal types instead of the widened primitive — useful for building precise union types from arrays/objects.
tip: Great for turning a list of strings into a literal union type.
code:
const sizes = ['sm', 'md', 'lg'] as const;
type Size = typeof sizes[number]; // 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg'

question: What is the non-null assertion operator (!)?
answer: The postfix ! tells the compiler 'this value is definitely not null/undefined here'. It removes the check but does not add a runtime guard — use it only when you're certain.
tip: Overusing ! defeats strictNullChecks — narrow properly when you can.
code: @ViewChild('el') el!: ElementRef; // asserted, assigned by Angular later

question: What is a discriminated (tagged) union?
answer: A union of object types that share a common literal tag field. Switching on that tag lets TypeScript narrow to the exact member, giving type-safe, exhaustive handling — great for state machines and Redux/NgRx actions.
tip: Add a `never` default case so the compiler errors if you forget a variant.

question: What are conditional types?
answer: Types that branch based on a condition: T extends U ? X : Y. They power much of the standard library (e.g. Exclude, ReturnType) and let generic types adapt to their inputs.
tip: Combine with `infer` to extract a type from within another.
code:
type NonNull<T> = T extends null | undefined ? never : T;
type A = NonNull<string | null>; // string

question: What does the infer keyword do?
answer: Inside a conditional type, infer captures a type from a position so you can reuse it — e.g. extract a function's return type or an array's element type.
tip: infer is how ReturnType<T> and Parameters<T> are implemented.
code:
type Return<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;
type R = Return<() => number>; // number

question: What is an index signature?
answer: Declares that an object can have arbitrary keys of a given type: { [key: string]: number }. Useful for dictionaries when keys aren't known ahead of time. Record<K, V> is the shorthand.
tip: Prefer Record<string, T> for readability over a raw index signature.
code:
interface Scores { [player: string]: number; }
const s: Scores = { alice: 10, bob: 7 };

question: What are tuple types?
answer: Fixed-length arrays with a known type per position — [string, number]. Support optional and rest elements, and labels for readability. React's useState return is a tuple.
tip: Tuples are how you type a function that returns a pair, like [value, setter].
code:
type Result = [ok: boolean, message: string];
const r: Result = [true, 'saved'];

question: What are function overloads?
answer: Multiple type signatures for one implementation, so callers get precise return types based on their arguments. Declare the overload signatures, then one compatible implementation signature.
tip: Prefer a union or generic if overloads get unwieldy — they're a last resort.

question: Abstract class vs interface in TypeScript.
answer: An abstract class can't be instantiated and may provide shared implementation plus abstract members subclasses must implement. An interface is purely a compile-time contract with no implementation and vanishes at runtime.
tip: Use an abstract class to share base logic; an interface just to enforce a shape.
code: abstract class Base { abstract load(): void; log() {} }

question: What does the satisfies operator do?
answer: (TS 4.9+) satisfies checks that a value matches a type without widening it — you keep the precise inferred literal types while still validating the shape. Best of both worlds vs a plain annotation.
tip: Use satisfies for config objects so keys/values keep their literal types.
code:
const routes = { home: '/', about: '/about' } satisfies Record<string, string>;
// routes.home is '/', not just string

question: What are template literal types?
answer: Types built from string literals using template syntax, enabling precise string unions derived from other types — e.g. event names or CSS units.
tip: Great for typing things like `on${Capitalize<Event>}` handler names.
code:
type Size = 'sm' | 'lg';
type Class = `btn-${Size}`; // 'btn-sm' | 'btn-lg'

question: How do you constrain generics (extends, keyof)?
answer: Use extends to require a shape, and keyof to restrict a parameter to an object's keys — enabling type-safe property access helpers.
tip: The typed getProp helper is a classic generics + keyof interview example.
